passages.explanation: Jesus uses a vine metaphor. He is the true vine and the Father is the gardener. Branches that don't bear fruit are cut off; branches that do bear fruit are pruned so they produce even more. The disciples are already clean because of Jesus' words. The instruction is to remain in him — a branch can't produce anything disconnected from the vine. Whoever remains in Jesus bears much fruit; apart from him they can do nothing. Anyone who doesn't remain is like a withered branch thrown into the fire. If they remain in him and his words remain in them, they can ask for whatever they want and it will happen.

The Father is glorified when they bear much fruit — that's how they prove they are Jesus' disciples. Just as the Father has loved Jesus, Jesus has loved them. He tells them to remain in his love. The way to remain is to keep his commandments, just as he keeps the Father's commandments and remains in the Father's love. He's telling them this so that his joy will be in them and their joy will be complete.

Jesus gives a specific commandment: love one another the way he has loved them. The greatest expression of love is laying down your life for your friends. They are his friends if they do what he commands. He no longer calls them servants — a servant doesn't know the master's business. He calls them friends because he has told them everything the Father told him. They didn't choose him; he chose them and appointed them to go out and bear lasting fruit. Whatever they ask the Father in his name will be given to them.

Jesus repeats the command to love one another, then shifts to what they should expect from the world. If the world hates them, they should know it hated him first. The world loves its own; they're hated because Jesus chose them out of the world. If they persecuted Jesus, they'll persecute his followers too. All of this happens because the world doesn't know the one who sent Jesus. If Jesus hadn't come and spoken to them, they wouldn't be guilty — but now they have no excuse. Hating Jesus is the same as hating the Father. If Jesus hadn't done works no one else had done, they wouldn't be guilty — but now they've seen and hated both him and the Father. This fulfils the Scripture: 'They hated me without a cause.'

Jesus says the Counsellor — the Spirit of truth — will come from the Father and will testify about Jesus. The disciples will also testify because they've been with him from the beginning. The Spirit's testimony and the disciples' testimony will work together.
